outcome_contract.why: Right now, if work gets stuck in the queue or errors start piling up, nobody knows until someone manually runs a report. This means problems can sit unnoticed for hours or days. Live monitoring would flag issues as they happen, so they get fixed before they snowball.
outcome_contract.intended_outcome_statement: Workflow queue health is continuously monitored with automatic alerts when thresholds are breached.

engineering_coverage.applicable: Clear exit codes (0=healthy, 1=alert, 2=error); structured JSON output with pass/warning/alert per metric
engineering_coverage.current_or_option_state: JSON response with same structure; interactive query
engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: Option A: define 3 exit states + JSON body
N/A for Option A; existing auth for B

engineering_coverage.applicable: Results to stdout + optional gitignored file for audit trail
engineering_coverage.current_or_option_state: No persistence — result is ephemeral in MCP response
engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: Option A: stdout primary, optional file for cron

engineering_coverage.applicable: Unit test the orchestration function; integration test with fixture data
engineering_coverage.current_or_option_state: Same unit tests plus MCP route test

engineering_coverage.applicable: Reads 2 files per invocation (~1.6MB queue-state + growing telemetry JSONL); metrics-runner handles missing files
engineering_coverage.current_or_option_state: Same, but MCP adds request overhead
engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: Both acceptable; telemetry growth is the shared concern

engineering_coverage.applicable: Add pnpm script; rollback = remove script
engineering_coverage.current_or_option_state: Add MCP tool; rollback = remove tool registration

stage_payload.decision_summary: The workflow queue has all the computation and alert logic needed for health monitoring — but no automated way to run it and surface results. This analysis selects the best approach for adding a lightweight health check that evaluates queue health and surfaces alerts.
goals: Select an approach that reuses existing metrics infrastructure (metrics-rollup, metrics-runner)
Define output contract and alert delivery mechanism
Keep the solution additive and minimal
constraints: No persistent server process — must work as a CLI invocation
Must not mutate source data files (queue-state.json, telemetry.jsonl)
Health check results go to a separate output file
assumptions: Current workflow volume makes periodic CLI checks sufficient

evaluation_criteria.Why it matters: Operator must be able to act on alerts quickly
Future alerting channels (MCP, email) should plug in easily

options_considered.Option: A: Thin CLI wrapper around metrics-runner + telemetry summary
options_considered.Description: New `workflow-health-check.ts` CLI that calls `runMetricsRollup()` for queue/cycle health (alerts via `action_records`) and `summarizeWorkflowStepTelemetry()` for workflow-step health, writes structured JSON result to stdout and optionally to a gitignored output file, exits with non-zero on alerts
options_considered.Upside: Maximum reuse; ~150-200 new lines; follows existing CLI pattern; cron-friendly exit codes
options_considered.Downside: No push notification — operator must check output or cron exit
options_considered.Key risks: Operator may miss alerts if cron isn't monitored

options_considered.Description: Add `workflow_health_check` tool to brikette MCP server that wraps same logic, accessible from BOS
options_considered.Upside: Interactive — operator can query health on demand; integrates with existing BOS workflow
options_considered.Downside: More integration surface; MCP server must be running; not cron-friendly; adds to MCP server scope
options_considered.Key risks: MCP server availability dependency; harder to test

options_considered.Description: Persistent process with polling loop, WebSocket updates, notification dispatch
options_considered.Downside: Way out of scope; requires server infrastructure; high implementation effort

chosen_approach.Recommendation: Option A — Thin CLI wrapper around metrics-runner
chosen_approach.Why this wins: Maximum code reuse (`runMetricsRollup()` provides queue/cycle metrics with alerts already evaluated as `action_records`; `summarizeWorkflowStepTelemetry()` provides workflow-step metrics), follows existing CLI patterns, cron-friendly with exit codes, simplest test surface.
chosen_approach.What it depends on: Two existing public functions: `runMetricsRollup()` from metrics-runner and `summarizeWorkflowStepTelemetry()` from workflow-telemetry. No private function exports needed — `action_records` on the rollup already contains evaluated alerts.

rejected_approaches: **Option B (MCP tool)** — viable but adds unnecessary integration complexity for the initial deliverable. The MCP server must be running, testing is harder, and it doesn't support cron scheduling. Better as a follow-on enhancement.
**Option C (Full monitoring service)** — eliminated. Over-engineering for current workflow volume (~540 queue entries). No persistent server infrastructure exists or is needed.

planning_focus: Create CLI entry point with pnpm script and exit code semantics
Output results to stdout (primary); optionally write to a gitignored file (e.g. `data/health-check-latest.json`) for cron audit trail — not in `docs/` to avoid git churn
sequencing_constraints: Health check function depends on both `runMetricsRollup()` and `summarizeWorkflowStepTelemetry()` (both already public)
CLI entry point depends on health check function
risks_to_carry_forward: Telemetry JSONL growth — plan should consider time-windowed reads if performance degrades
Cycle telemetry and workflow-step telemetry are in the same JSONL file but parsed by different functions — health check must call both
